sections.title: Use
rows: To indicate that a subject arranges for somebody else to perform a service or task.
The manager **has** her office **redecorated** every year.

sections.title: Tense / modal
rows: The manager **has** her office **redecorated** every year.
She **is having** the walls **painted** at the moment.
She **was having** the computers **repaired** when I visited.
She **will have** a new sofa **delivered**. / She **would have** new curtains **put up**.
She **is going to have** a new printer **installed**.
She might stop **having** the office **cleaned** so often.

exercises.title: A: Complete the sentences
exercises.instructions: Use the causative form of have and the verb in brackets.
exercises.studentInstruction: Use the causative form of have and the verb in brackets.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is past causative, so the correct answer is "had, designed".

wrongOptions.design: The bare infinitive (design) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Present perfect does not combine with finished past-time words like yesterday or last week.
explanationContent.usefulTip: A finished past-time expression usually requires the past simple, not a perfect tense.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Past simple is used for finished actions at a specific past time — watch irregular past forms.

items.sentence: He ...... his suit ...... for the ceremony tomorrow. (clean)

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is present continuous causative, so the correct answer is "is having, cleaned".

wrongOptions.clean: The bare infinitive (clean) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check subject–verb agreement, tense, and whether the verb form matches the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Time words like already, yet, since, and for are strong signals for present perfect.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Conjugate (clean) to match the subject and the time clues in the sentence.

items.sentence: We ...... our air conditioner ...... before summer. (service)

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is future causative, so the correct answer is "are going to have, serviced".

wrongOptions.service: The bare infinitive (service) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check subject–verb agreement, tense, and whether the verb form matches the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the subject and any time expressions before you conjugate — they decide tense and agreement.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Choose the future form that matches whether the sentence predicts, plans, or arranges.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is present simple causative, so the correct answer is "has, done".

wrongOptions.do: The bare infinitive (do) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple misses the link to now — present perfect needs have/has + past participle.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Time words like already, yet, since, and for are strong signals for present perfect.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect (have/has + past participle) links past actions to now or unfinished time.

items.sentence: I ...... my phone screen ...... before I sold it. (replace)

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is past perfect causative, so the correct answer is "had had, replaced".

wrongOptions.had, replaced: Without had, the earlier-past relationship is lost.
wrongOptions.replace: The bare infinitive (replace) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple only shows one past event — use had + past participle for the earlier action.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the subject and any time expressions before you conjugate — they decide tense and agreement.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Past perfect (had + past participle) shows the earlier of two past actions.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is should + causative, so the correct answer is "have, translated".

wrongOptions.translate: The bare infinitive (translate) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check subject–verb agreement, tense, and whether the verb form matches the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the subject and any time expressions before you conjugate — they decide tense and agreement.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Modals and semi-modals express ability, obligation, permission, or possibility — match the nuance in the sentence.

items.displayPrompt: A plumber repaired our shower yesterday.

cues: A plumber repaired our shower yesterday.

items.answer: We had our shower repaired yesterday.
acceptedAnswers: We had our shower repaired yesterday.
items.explanation: Causative rewrite → **We had our shower repaired** yesterday.

items.displayPrompt: A stylist is colouring Nina's hair now.

cues: A stylist is colouring Nina's hair now.

items.answer: Nina is having her hair coloured now.
acceptedAnswers: Nina is having her hair coloured now.
items.explanation: Present continuous → **is having her hair coloured** now.

items.displayPrompt: A company will deliver their new sofa tomorrow.

cues: A company will deliver their new sofa tomorrow.

items.answer: They will have their new sofa delivered tomorrow.
acceptedAnswers: They will have their new sofa delivered tomorrow.
items.explanation: Future causative → **will have their new sofa delivered**.

items.displayPrompt: A technician is updating his computer software.

cues: A technician is updating his computer software.

items.answer: He is having his computer software updated.
acceptedAnswers: He is having his computer software updated.
items.explanation: Present continuous → **is having his computer software updated**.

items.displayPrompt: A gardener trims their trees every spring.

cues: A gardener trims their trees every spring.

items.answer: They have their trees trimmed every spring.
acceptedAnswers: They have their trees trimmed every spring.
items.explanation: Habitual causative → **have their trees trimmed** every spring.

items.displayPrompt: An optician tested my eyes last week.

cues: An optician tested my eyes last week.

items.answer: I had my eyes tested last week.
acceptedAnswers: I had my eyes tested last week.
items.explanation: Past causative → **had my eyes tested** last week.

exercises.title: C: Rewrite using the causative
exercises.instructions: Complete the second sentence using the causative. Use no more than four words.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete the second sentence using the causative. Use no more than four words.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is past causative, so the correct answer is "had".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Present perfect does not combine with finished past-time words like yesterday or last week.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Past simple is used for finished actions at a specific past time.

items.sentence: They are ...... their flat decorated next month.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is future, so the correct answer is "going to have".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "going to have", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: He ...... his teeth cleaned last Friday.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is past causative, so the correct answer is "had".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: They ...... their car washed every week.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is habit, so the correct answer is "have".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple is wrong when the sentence links past to now (for, since, already, yet, ever).
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect links a past action to the present or unfinished time.

items.sentence: She ...... her passport photo taken yesterday.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is past causative, so the correct answer is "had".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Present perfect does not combine with finished past-time words like yesterday or last week.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Past simple is used for finished actions at a specific past time.

items.sentence: They are ...... their company logo made.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is present continuous, so the correct answer is "having".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.
